title: store-memory Skill
context: Create a pi agent skill that allows the agent to autonomously dump conceptual observations to ~/Documents/wiki/raw/inbox/ as timestamped markdown files with minimal frontmatter. The agent decides autonomously when to store. Content boundary: general/conceptual knowledge only (not code-specific patterns, which go to .claude/rules/). The ingest pipeline handles curation later.
architecture: Skills live in pi/.pi/agent/skills/<name>/SKILL.md (Markdown instruction files). Extensions live in pi/.pi/agent/extensions/<name>/ (TypeScript code registering tools/commands). For store-memory, the skill instructs the agent to use the write tool (or a bash script) to create files in raw/inbox/. No downstream processing — the obsidian-wiki-ingest skill handles that separately.

red.description: Write test that calls createMemoryNote with title, content, and tags arguments and verifies a markdown file is created in ~/Documents/wiki/raw/inbox/ with YYYY-MM-DD-descriptive-slug.md naming, correct YAML frontmatter (title, created as ISO 8601, tags as array), and content body

green.description: Implement createMemoryNote library function and CLI wrapper that writes the file. CLI accepts --title, --tags, content from stdin or positional args

red.description: Write test that validates SKILL.md exists at pi/.pi/agent/skills/store-memory/SKILL.md, has valid YAML frontmatter with required fields (name, description), and contains sections for trigger, format, usage, and content boundary rules

green.description: Write the SKILL.md with proper structure

design.currentState: The wiki has a raw/inbox/ directory at ~/Documents/wiki/raw/inbox/ that is currently empty. Raw/ is for user-placed source documents with YAML frontmatter. Existing frontmatter conventions (from raw/notes/): title, source, author, published, created, description, tags. Pi skills live in pi/.pi/agent/skills/<name>/SKILL.md — simple markdown instruction files. Extensions (pi/.pi/agent/extensions/<name>/) register tools/commands in TypeScript. The wiki-stash extension already writes to wiki/concepts|entities|synthesis — distinct from raw/inbox/ dumping. The obsidian-wiki-ingest skill processes inbox contents into the wiki structure. No existing extension or skill for raw/inbox/ writing exists.
design.desiredState: A store-memory skill (pi/.pi/agent/skills/store-memory/SKILL.md) that instructs the agent: when to store (autonomously, any moment), what to store (conceptual knowledge only, not code patterns), and how to write to raw/inbox/ with correct format. Files created follow hybrid naming: YYYY-MM-DD-descriptive-slug.md. Frontmatter: title, created (ISO 8601), tags (array). The skill tells the agent to use the write tool (or bash) directly — no extension needed. The skill is listed in pi/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md's skill registry.
design.patterns: Skill format (from commit/SKILL.md): YAML frontmatter (name, description) + markdown body with ## sections for workflow steps and usage. Skills reference tools via backtick-bash blocks (e.g. ```bash\nwiki-search ...\n```). Skills are invoked via /skill:<name>. Raw/ frontmatter conventions: notes use title, source, author, published, created, description, tags. For agent-authored memories: title (descriptive), created (ISO 8601 timestamp), tags (kebab-case array). File naming: lowercase-hyphen-separated with date prefix.
design.decisions: Trigger: agent decides autonomously, no permission asked. Content: general/conceptual knowledge → wiki; code patterns → .claude/rules/. Frontmatter: minimal (title, created, tags). Naming: YYYY-MM-DD-descriptive-slug.md. Destination: ~/Documents/wiki/raw/inbox/. Integration: independent from commit/ingest skills. No extension needed — agent uses write/bash tools directly.
design.questions: What test framework validates this? Skills are markdown, not TypeScript — so there's no standard TDD loop. Options: (1) create a small CLI helper script in ~/.local/bin/ that handles the file creation and test that, (2) create a lightweight extension that registers a store_memory tool (testable with bun test, matches existing patterns), or (3) test just the SKILL.md structure (frontmatter validity, content conventions) as a 'soft' validation. Which approach fits?
